defined_by.meaning: source item is defined by target definition

introduces.meaning: source field, text, theorem, or construction introduces target item

assumes.meaning: source result or proof assumes target assumption/property/structure

requires.meaning: source item requires target to be stated or constructed

uses.meaning: source proof, theorem, application, or step uses target method/result/item

implies.meaning: source result logically implies target result

equivalent_to.meaning: source and target are mathematically equivalent

subtype_of.meaning: source structure/object is a subtype of target

instance_of.meaning: source example/object is an instance of target class/structure/property

constructed_from.meaning: source construction/object is built from target item

induces.meaning: source item canonically induces target item

has_example.meaning: source class/result has target as example

has_counterexample.meaning: source item is linked to target counterexample/non-example, or a counterexample source is attached to its target class when the label says so

shows_necessity_of.meaning: source counterexample demonstrates that target assumption/property cannot be dropped

motivates.meaning: source item motivates target item pedagogically or historically

prerequisite_for.meaning: source is useful or necessary to learn before target

applied_to.meaning: source result/method is applied to target application/context

graph.label: Functional Analysis mini knowledge graph
design_notes: Trees are extracted views; the stored object is a directed typed multigraph.
Definitions, assumptions, proofs, proof steps, examples, and counterexamples are first-class nodes.
Theorem assumptions, statement definitions, and proof dependencies use different dependency classes.
Terminology nodes distinguish raw functions/maps from operators: every operator is a function, but operator carries structured-space context in functional analysis.

items.label: Functional Analysis
items.definition: Study of normed, topological, and infinite-dimensional linear spaces and their continuous linear maps.
items.intuition: Linear algebra with topology, completeness, duality, and limits turned on.

items.label: Vector space
items.definition: A set with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying the vector space axioms over a field.
items.intuition: The algebraic stage on which linear combinations make sense.

items.label: Metric space
items.formal_statement: (M,d) with d:M\times M\to[0,\infty), d(x,y)=0\Leftrightarrow x=y, d(x,y)=d(y,x), d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z).
items.definition: A set equipped with a distance function satisfying positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
items.intuition: A space where convergence and Cauchy sequences can be measured.

items.label: Cauchy sequence
items.statement: A sequence in a metric space is Cauchy if its tail terms become arbitrarily close to one another.
items.formal_statement: Let $(M,d)$ be a metric space and let $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a sequence in $M$. The sequence, $(x_n)$ is Cauchy iff, $$\forall \varepsilon>0\,\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,\forall m,n\ge N:\ d(x_m,x_n)<\varepsilon$$
items.definition: A Cauchy sequence is a sequence in a metric space whose sufficiently late terms are pairwise arbitrarily close.
items.intuition: The sequence stabilizes internally: far enough along the sequence, its terms cluster together, whether or not the space actually contains a limiting point.
notation: $$\begin{aligned} &(x_n)\text{ is Cauchy}\\ &\iff \forall \varepsilon>0\,\exists N\in\mathbb{N}\,\forall m,n\ge N:\ d(x_m,x_n)<\varepsilon \\ &\iff d(x_m,x_n)\to 0\text{ as }m,n\to\infty \end{aligned}$$
differentia: Sufficiently late terms are pairwise arbitrarily close.
The condition compares tail terms to each other rather than to a specified limit.
equivalent_definitions: For every $\varepsilon>0$, some tail of the sequence has diameter less than $\varepsilon$.
In a normed vector space, $(x_n)$ is Cauchy iff $\|x_m-x_n\|\to 0$ as $m,n\to\infty$.

items.label: Topological vector space
items.definition: A vector space with a topology making addition and scalar multiplication continuous.
items.intuition: A vector space where limits are compatible with linear operations.

items.label: Normed vector space
items.statement: A normed vector space is a vector space equipped with a norm.
items.formal_statement: A normed vector space is a pair $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ where $X$ is a vector space over $\mathbb{K}\in\{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}\}$ and $\|\cdot\|:X\to[0,\infty)$ satisfies $\|x\|=0\iff x=0$, $\|\lambda x\|=|\lambda|\|x\|$, and $\|x+y\|\le \|x\|+\|y\|$ for all $x,y\in X$ and $\lambda\in\mathbb{K}$.
items.definition: A normed vector space is a vector space together with a norm, giving each vector a length and inducing a metric by $d(x,y)=\|x-y\|$.
items.intuition: A normed space is linear algebra with a compatible notion of size. Once vectors have lengths, one can measure distances, define limits, study continuity, and ask whether Cauchy sequences converge.

items.label: Banach space
items.formal_statement: A normed space X such that every norm-Cauchy sequence converges in X.
items.definition: A normed vector space complete in the metric induced by its norm.
items.intuition: A normed space with no missing limits.

items.label: Inner product space
items.formal_statement: (H,\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle) with positive-definite sesquilinear inner product.
items.definition: A vector space equipped with an inner product inducing a norm.
items.intuition: A vector space where angles and orthogonality make sense.

items.label: Hilbert space
items.formal_statement: An inner product space H complete under \|x\|=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle}.
items.definition: A complete inner product space.
items.intuition: Euclidean geometry after passing to infinite dimensions.

items.label: Locally convex space
items.definition: A topological vector space whose topology has a local base at zero consisting of convex sets.
items.intuition: A TVS with enough convex geometry for separation theorems.

items.label: Dual space
items.definition: The Banach space of continuous linear functionals on a normed vector space.
items.intuition: The space of all continuous scalar probes on X.

items.label: Norm
items.formal_statement: $$\|x\|\ge0, \|x\|=0\Leftrightarrow x=0, \|\lambda x\|=|\lambda|\|x\|, \|x+y\|\le\|x\|+\|y\|.$$
items.definition: A function assigning lengths to vectors, satisfying positivity, homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.
items.intuition: Length compatible with linear structure.
differentia: positive definite
absolutely homogeneous
subadditive

items.label: Metric induced by a norm
items.definition: The metric d(x,y)=||x-y|| associated to a norm.
items.intuition: Distance is length of displacement.

items.label: Topology induced by a metric
items.definition: The topology generated by open metric balls.
items.intuition: Open sets are built from distance balls.

items.label: Completeness
items.formal_statement: (x_n) Cauchy in X implies there exists x in X with x_n -> x.
items.definition: Every Cauchy sequence converges to a point of the space.
items.intuition: Cauchy approximation cannot converge outside the space.

items.label: Compactness
items.definition: Every open cover admits a finite subcover.
items.intuition: Topological finiteness; in metric spaces often sequential compactness.

items.label: Weak topology
items.formal_statement: \sigma(X,X^*) is generated by seminorms x\mapsto |x^*(x)|, x^*\in X^*.
items.definition: The coarsest topology on X making every functional in X* continuous.
items.intuition: Convergence is tested only by continuous scalar probes.

items.label: Weak-star topology
items.formal_statement: \sigma(X^*,X).
items.definition: The coarsest topology on X* making every evaluation map x* -> x*(x) continuous for x in X.
items.intuition: Pointwise convergence on the predual X.

items.label: Boundedness
items.definition: A set or operator is bounded when its size is controlled by a finite constant in the relevant norm.
items.intuition: Uniform finite control.

items.label: Continuity
items.definition: Preimages of open sets are open; for linear maps between normed spaces this is equivalent to boundedness.
items.intuition: Small input changes produce small output changes.

items.label: Linear operator
items.formal_statement: Let $X,Y$ be vector spaces over $\mathbb K$. A map $T:X\to Y$ is a linear operator iff $T(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha Tx+\beta Ty$ for all $x,y\in X$ and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb K$.
items.definition: A linear operator is an operator between vector spaces that preserves addition and scalar multiplication.
items.intuition: An operator that respects superposition: transform after combining equals combine after transforming.

items.label: Linear functional
items.formal_statement: f:X\to K linear.
items.definition: A linear map from a vector space to its scalar field.
items.intuition: A scalar measurement of vectors.
differentia: codomain is scalar field

items.label: Bounded linear operator
items.statement: A bounded linear operator is a linear map between normed vector spaces whose output norm is controlled by a constant multiple of the input norm.
items.formal_statement: Let $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ and $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ be normed vector spaces. A linear map $T:X\to Y$ is bounded iff $\exists C>0\,\forall x\in X:\ \|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X$.
items.definition: A bounded linear operator is a linear operator $T:X\to Y$ such that $\|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X$ for all $x\in X$ and some constant $C>0$.
items.intuition: The operator may stretch vectors, but only by a uniform linear factor. It cannot amplify small inputs into disproportionately large outputs, because apparently analysis wanted the word bounded to mean something subtler than bounded.
items.notation:
$$\begin{aligned}
T\in L(X,Y)
&\iff T:X\to Y\text{ is linear and }\exists C>0\ \forall x\in X:\ \|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X,\\
\|T\|
&:=\sup_{\|x\|_X\le 1}\|Tx\|_Y<\infty.
\end{aligned}$$
differentia: There exists a constant $C>0$ such that $\|Tx\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X$ for all $x\in X$.
Equivalently, the operator norm $\|T\|$ is finite.
Equivalently, $T$ maps bounded subsets of $X$ to bounded subsets of $Y$.
equivalent_definitions: $T$ maps bounded sets in $X$ to bounded sets in $Y$.

items.label: Bounded function on a domain
items.statement: A function is bounded on its domain if its entire image lies inside one ball of the codomain.
items.formal_statement: Let $X$ be a set and let $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ be a normed vector space. A function $f:X\to Y$ is bounded on $X$ iff $\exists M>0\,\forall x\in X:\ \|f(x)\|_Y\le M$.
items.definition: A function $f:X\to Y$ is bounded on $X$ if all values of $f$ have norm bounded above by a single constant.
items.intuition: The whole image $f(X)$ fits inside one ball centered at $0$ in $Y$.
items.notation:
$$\begin{aligned}
f:X\to Y\text{ bounded on }X
&\iff \exists M>0\ \forall x\in X:\ \|f(x)\|_Y\le M,\\
f(X)&\subseteq B_Y(0,M).
\end{aligned}$$
items.genus: function into a normed vector space
differentia: There exists $M>0$ such that $\|f(x)\|_Y\le M$ for every $x\in X$.
The image $f(X)$ is contained in a ball of finite radius in $Y$.

items.label: Continuous linear operator
items.definition: A linear operator that is continuous between the given topologies.
items.intuition: The same object as a bounded linear operator in normed spaces.

items.label: Operator norm
items.definition: The norm of a bounded linear operator given by its largest action on the unit ball.
items.intuition: Worst-case amplification factor.
differentia: defined on bounded linear operators

items.label: Compact operator
items.formal_statement: T:X->Y compact iff T(B_X) has compact closure in Y.
items.definition: A bounded linear operator that maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets.
items.intuition: An infinite-dimensional operator behaving partly like a finite-rank operator.
differentia: image of bounded sets is relatively compact

items.label: Adjoint operator
items.definition: For T:X->Y, the map T*:Y*->X* defined by composition with T.
items.intuition: Move T from vectors to scalar probes by precomposition.

items.label: Self-adjoint operator
items.formal_statement: T=T^*, equivalently <Tx,y>=<x,Ty>.
items.definition: An operator on a Hilbert space equal to its Hilbert-space adjoint.
items.intuition: Infinite-dimensional analogue of a Hermitian matrix.
differentia: equals its adjoint

items.label: Sublinear functional
items.formal_statement: p(\alpha x)=\alpha p(x) for \alpha>=0 and p(x+y)<=p(x)+p(y).
items.definition: A positively homogeneous and subadditive real-valued map on a real vector space.
items.intuition: A one-sided convex bound for a linear functional.
differentia: positive homogeneous
subadditive

items.label: Closed graph
items.definition: The graph {(x,Tx)} is closed in X x Y.
items.intuition: If x_n -> x and Tx_n -> y, then y=Tx.

items.label: Closed unit ball
items.definition: The set of points of norm at most one.
items.intuition: The basic bounded test set for normed-space geometry.

items.label: Spectrum
items.formal_statement: sigma(T)={lambda: T-lambda I is not bijective with bounded inverse}.
items.definition: The set of scalars lambda for which T-lambda I is not invertible in the appropriate operator algebra.
items.intuition: The infinite-dimensional replacement for eigenvalues.

items.label: Orthonormal basis
items.definition: A maximal orthonormal set whose closed linear span is the Hilbert space.
items.intuition: Coordinate axes for Hilbert space.

items.label: Projection operator
items.definition: A linear operator P satisfying P^2=P.
items.intuition: A map that keeps one component and kills another.

items.label: Axiom of Choice
items.statement: Every family of nonempty sets admits a choice function.
items.intuition: A global choice principle enabling maximal-object arguments.

items.label: Zorn's lemma
items.statement: If every chain in a partially ordered set has an upper bound, then the poset has a maximal element.
items.intuition: The maximal-extension engine behind Hahn-Banach.

items.label: Baire category theorem
items.statement: A complete metric space is not a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets.
items.formal_statement: If X is complete metric and X=union F_n with F_n closed, then some F_n has nonempty interior.
items.intuition: Completeness prevents a space from being too thin.
hypotheses: X is a complete metric space
items.conclusion: Some closed set in a countable closed cover has nonempty interior.

items.label: Diagonal argument
items.definition: A method selecting subsequences or objects along a diagonal to satisfy countably many conditions.
items.intuition: Build one object that survives infinitely many requirements.

items.label: Density argument
items.definition: Prove on a dense subset, then extend by continuity.
items.intuition: Control the whole space from a dense core.

items.label: Compactness argument
items.definition: Use finite subcovers, convergent subsequences, or compactness to extract limiting structure.
items.intuition: Turn infinite behavior into finite or convergent behavior.

items.label: Duality argument
items.definition: Translate a problem about vectors or operators into a problem about linear functionals or adjoints.
items.intuition: Probe objects by scalar-valued tests.

items.label: Approximation argument
items.definition: Approximate difficult objects by simpler ones and pass to a limit.
items.intuition: Prove first for finite/simple pieces, then complete.

items.label: Scalar field real or complex
items.statement: The scalar field is R or C.
items.intuition: The usual analytic scalar fields.

items.label: Domain is Banach
items.statement: The domain space is complete in its norm.
items.intuition: Baire-category arguments require completeness of the domain.

items.label: Codomain is Banach
items.statement: The codomain space is complete in its norm.
items.intuition: Closed graph/open mapping statements need Banach codomain.

items.label: Pointwise bounded family
items.statement: For each x, the set {||T_i x||} is bounded.
items.intuition: Each vector is controlled separately before uniform boundedness upgrades the control.

items.label: Surjective operator
items.statement: The linear operator maps X onto Y.
items.intuition: Open mapping requires every target vector to have a preimage.

items.label: Operator has closed graph
items.statement: The graph of T is closed in X x Y.
items.intuition: Sequential limit compatibility replaces an a priori boundedness estimate.

items.label: Dual unit ball with weak-star topology
items.statement: The closed unit ball of X* is considered with sigma(X*,X).
items.intuition: Banach-Alaoglu is a compactness theorem for this specific topology.

items.label: Compact self-adjoint operator
items.statement: The operator is compact and self-adjoint on a Hilbert space.
items.intuition: Compactness gives eigenvalue accumulation control; self-adjointness gives orthogonality.

items.label: Hahn-Banach theorem
items.statement: A dominated linear functional on a subspace extends to the whole space without increasing its bound.
items.formal_statement: If f<=p on V subset E, p sublinear, then f extends to F:E->R with F<=p; normed-space versions preserve norm.
items.intuition: Scalar probes defined on a subspace can be extended globally.
hypotheses: V is a subspace of E
f is linear and dominated by p on V
items.conclusion: There exists a linear extension to E dominated by p; in normed spaces the extension can preserve norm.

variants.label: Norm-preserving Hahn-Banach
variants.conclusion: There is F in X* extending f with ||F||=||f||.

variants.label: Geometric separation form
hypotheses: disjoint convex sets under separation assumptions
variants.conclusion: A continuous linear functional separates the sets.

items.label: Banach-Steinhaus theorem / Uniform Boundedness Principle
items.statement: A pointwise bounded family of bounded linear operators on a Banach space is uniformly bounded in operator norm.
items.formal_statement: If X Banach and {T_i} subset L(X,Y) with sup_i ||T_i x||<infty for each x, then sup_i ||T_i||<infty.
items.intuition: Pointwise control plus completeness forces uniform operator control.
hypotheses: {T_i x} is bounded for every x in X

items.label: Open Mapping Theorem
items.statement: A surjective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces maps open sets to open sets.
items.formal_statement: If $T:X \to Y$ is bounded linear and onto, with $X,Y$ Banach, then $T$ is open. $$\forall U \subset X \text{ open}, T(U) \subset Y \text{ is open}.$$
items.intuition: A complete linear surjection cannot crush all neighborhoods.

items.label: Closed Graph Theorem
items.statement: A linear operator between Banach spaces is bounded if its graph is closed.
items.formal_statement: If $X,Y$ are Banach and $T:X \to Y$ is linear with graph(T) closed in $X \times Y$, then $T$ is bounded. $$\exists C<\infty \text{ such that } ||Tx|| \leq C||x|| \text{ for all } x \in X.$$
items.intuition: A graph-compatible limit rule forces norm continuity.

items.label: Banach-Alaoglu Theorem
items.statement: The closed unit ball of the dual space is compact in the weak-star topology.
items.formal_statement: B_{X^*} is compact in sigma(X^*,X).
items.intuition: Pointwise bounded scalar probes have compactness in the weakest natural topology.

items.label: Hilbert projection theorem
items.statement: Every point in a Hilbert space has a unique nearest point in a nonempty closed convex subset.
items.formal_statement: For closed convex C subset H and x in H, there is unique c in C minimizing ||x-c||.
items.intuition: Orthogonal projection is the engine of Hilbert space geometry.

items.label: Hilbert-space Riesz Representation Theorem
items.statement: Every continuous linear functional on a Hilbert space is inner product with a unique vector.
items.formal_statement: For every f in H*, there exists unique y in H such that f(x)=<x,y> for all x in H and ||f||=||y||.
items.intuition: The dual of a Hilbert space is the Hilbert space itself, anti-linearly or linearly depending on convention.
items.conclusion: There exists unique y in H representing f by the inner product.

items.label: Spectral Theorem
items.statement: A compact self-adjoint operator on a Hilbert space admits an orthonormal eigenbasis for the closure of its range and a spectral decomposition.
items.formal_statement: If T is compact self-adjoint on H, then nonzero spectrum consists of real eigenvalues with finite-dimensional eigenspaces accumulating only at 0, and H decomposes orthogonally into eigenspaces plus ker(T).
items.intuition: Compact self-adjoint operators behave like diagonal Hermitian matrices.
items.conclusion: T has real spectral data and an orthogonal decomposition by eigenspaces.

variants.label: Bounded self-adjoint spectral theorem
hypotheses: T bounded self-adjoint on Hilbert space
variants.conclusion: T admits a projection-valued spectral measure.

items.label: Bounded Inverse Theorem
items.statement: A bijective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces has bounded inverse.
items.intuition: Banach-space bijections are automatically topological isomorphisms.

items.label: Proof of Hahn-Banach by Zorn
items.statement: Extend dominated functionals by maximal extension.
items.intuition: Build the largest possible extension; maximality forces full domain.
items.strategy: Partially order dominated extensions, use Zorn to get a maximal one, then prove maximal domain is all of E.

items.label: HB step 1: order extensions
items.statement: Create a poset of admissible dominated extensions.
items.claim: Dominated extensions form a partially ordered set under extension.
items.justification: Restriction compatibility defines the order; domination is preserved by inclusion.

items.label: HB step 2: chain upper bound
items.statement: Unify a chain of compatible extensions.
items.claim: Every chain has an upper bound given by the union functional.
items.justification: Total ordering makes the union single-valued and linear.

items.label: HB step 3: maximal extension
items.statement: Apply Zorn to get a maximal dominated extension.
items.justification: Zorn applies because every chain has an upper bound.

items.label: HB step 4: one-dimensional extension contradiction
items.statement: If the maximal domain is not all of E, extend by one new vector.
items.claim: Maximality forces the extension domain to be E.
items.justification: The sublinear inequalities give a nonempty interval for the new scalar value.

items.label: Proof of Uniform Boundedness by Baire
items.statement: Use Baire on closed sets where the family is uniformly bounded pointwise.
items.intuition: One ball gets uniform control, then homogeneity spreads it to the unit ball.
items.strategy: Define E_n={x: sup_i ||T_i x||<=n}; use Baire to find E_n with interior; shift and scale to bound operator norms.

items.label: UBP step 1: closed control sets
items.statement: Define E_n={x: sup_i ||T_i x||<=n}.
items.claim: Each E_n is closed and union_n E_n = X.
items.justification: Continuity gives closedness; pointwise boundedness gives the union.

items.label: UBP step 2: Baire gives an interior ball
items.statement: Apply Baire category to the closed cover X=union E_n.
items.claim: Some E_N contains a nonempty open ball.
items.justification: X is Banach, hence complete metric.

items.label: UBP step 3: rescale to unit ball
items.statement: Use linearity to move the interior ball to a bound on the unit ball.
items.justification: Uniform control on a ball plus linearity controls all vectors of norm at most one.

items.label: Proof of Open Mapping Theorem by Baire
items.statement: Use Baire to show the image of a unit ball absorbs a neighborhood of zero.
items.intuition: Surjectivity plus completeness forces a ball in the image.
items.strategy: Cover Y by scaled closures of T(B_X), use Baire to get interior, then use an approximation series to remove closure.

items.label: Proof of Closed Graph via Open Mapping
items.statement: Make the graph into a Banach space and apply open mapping to the projection.
items.intuition: Closed graph is a complete domain in disguise.
items.strategy: Equip graph(T) with product norm; projection to X is bijective and bounded, so inverse is bounded; T follows bounded.

items.label: Proof of Banach-Alaoglu by product compactness
items.statement: Embed the dual unit ball into a product of compact scalar disks and close it.
items.intuition: Weak-star compactness is product compactness plus linear constraints.
items.strategy: Use Tychonoff/product compactness for pointwise bounded scalar values and show the weak-star unit ball is closed.

items.label: Proof of Hilbert Riesz representation
items.statement: Use the kernel of a functional and orthogonal projection.
items.intuition: A functional measures exactly the component along one vector.
items.strategy: For nonzero f, decompose H into ker(f) and its orthogonal complement, then identify the representing vector.

items.label: Proof of spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators
items.statement: Extract eigenvectors by compactness, then diagonalize orthogonally by induction/approximation.
items.intuition: Compactness gives eigenvectors; self-adjointness makes different eigenspaces orthogonal.
items.strategy: Use variational/compactness arguments to get eigenvectors, restrict to orthogonal complements, and complete the orthonormal decomposition.

items.label: ell^p spaces
items.formal_statement: ell^p={x=(x_n): sum |x_n|^p<infty}, 1<=p<infty; ell^infty bounded sequences.
items.definition: Sequence spaces of p-summable scalar sequences, with p-norm.
items.intuition: Discrete model Banach spaces.

items.label: L^p spaces
items.formal_statement: L^p(\Omega)= {f: integral |f|^p < infinity}/a.e. equality.
items.definition: Equivalence classes of p-integrable measurable functions.
items.intuition: Function-space analogue of ell^p.

items.label: C(K)
items.formal_statement: C(K) with ||f||_infty=sup_{x in K}|f(x)|.
items.definition: Continuous scalar-valued functions on a compact Hausdorff space K with supremum norm.
items.intuition: Continuous functions as a Banach space.

items.label: c_0
items.formal_statement: c_0={x in ell^infty: x_n -> 0}.
items.definition: The Banach space of scalar sequences converging to zero with the supremum norm.
items.intuition: A canonical non-reflexive Banach space.

items.label: ell^2 as Hilbert space
items.formal_statement: ell^2 with <x,y>=sum_n x_n overline{y_n}.
items.definition: The square-summable sequence space with inner product <x,y>=sum x_n conjugate(y_n).
items.intuition: The canonical separable infinite-dimensional Hilbert space.

items.label: Incomplete normed spaces as non-examples of Banach spaces
items.definition: Normed spaces with Cauchy sequences that do not converge inside the space.
items.intuition: Shows completeness is not cosmetic in Banach-space theorems.

items.label: Separation of points by functionals
items.statement: Hahn-Banach gives, for x != 0, a continuous functional x* with x*(x) != 0.
items.intuition: Dual functionals can detect nonzero vectors.

items.label: Pointwise limits of bounded operators
items.statement: Uniform boundedness shows pointwise limits of bounded linear operators on Banach domains are bounded under pointwise convergence.
items.intuition: A limit operator inherits boundedness from pointwise convergence plus completeness.

items.label: Weak compactness results
items.statement: Banach-Alaoglu is the compactness source behind weak and weak-star compactness arguments.
items.intuition: Compactness appears after weakening the topology.

items.label: Operator spectrum analysis
items.statement: Spectral theorems reduce suitable operator questions to scalar spectral data.
items.intuition: Diagonalize the operator conceptually, then analyze eigenvalues/spectrum.

items.label: Function / map
items.statement: A function is a rule assigning to each element of a domain exactly one element of a codomain.
items.formal_statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is a relation such that $\forall x\in X\,\exists! y\in Y:\ y=f(x)$.
items.definition: A function, also called a map or mapping, is an assignment from a domain $X$ to a codomain $Y$ with exactly one output value in $Y$ for each input value in $X$.
items.intuition: The bare mathematical arrow: inputs in one set are assigned outputs in another set, with no algebraic or topological structure assumed.
assumptions: Each $x\in X$ has exactly one assigned value $f(x)\in Y$.

metadata.source: standard mathematical terminology; compatible with the existing functional_analysis graph

differentia: Every input in the domain has an output.
The output is unique.
No linearity, continuity, boundedness, or topology is assumed by the word function alone.
equivalent_definitions: A single-valued total relation from $X$ to $Y$.

items.label: Operator
items.statement: In functional analysis, an operator is a map between structured spaces, usually vector, normed, Banach, or Hilbert spaces.
items.formal_statement: An operator is typically a map $T:X\to Y$ where $X,Y$ carry mathematical structure; in functional analysis $X,Y$ are usually vector or normed spaces. If additionally $T(\alpha x+\beta y)=\alpha Tx+\beta Ty$, then $T$ is a linear operator.
items.definition: An operator is a function or map acting between structured spaces. In functional analysis, the term usually signals that the domain and codomain are vector spaces or topological vector spaces, and often that the map is linear unless explicitly stated otherwise.
items.intuition: A function wearing structure: it is still an assignment, but the word operator tells the reader to care about vector-space, norm, topology, or Hilbert-space structure.
assumptions: The domain and codomain usually carry additional structure.
In functional analysis, linearity is common but should not be assumed unless stated or conventional in the surrounding text.

metadata.source: standard functional-analysis convention; compatible with Bowers-Kalton notation L(X,Y)

differentia: Acts between structured mathematical spaces.
In functional analysis, the relevant structure is usually vector-space, normed-space, Banach-space, or Hilbert-space structure.
Often linear in functional-analysis contexts, but the term operator alone is not universally synonymous with linear operator.
equivalent_definitions: A structured map between mathematical spaces.
In many functional-analysis texts: a map, often linear, between vector spaces or normed spaces.

items.label: Nonlinear operator
items.statement: A nonlinear operator is an operator that is not linear.
items.formal_statement: A map $F:X\to Y$ is nonlinear if there exist $x,y\in X$ or $\lambda\in\mathbb K$ such that $F(x+y)\ne F(x)+F(y)$ or $F(\lambda x)\ne \lambda F(x)$.
items.definition: A nonlinear operator is a map between structured spaces that fails linearity.
items.intuition: Still an operator because it acts on structured spaces; not a linear operator because it refuses to respect superposition.
assumptions: $X$ and $Y$ are structured spaces, usually vector or normed spaces.

items.label: Semantic strength: function versus operator
items.statement: The term function is more general/weaker than operator; operator is more specific/stronger in functional-analysis usage.
items.formal_statement: $\{\text{operators}\}\subseteq \{\text{functions/maps}\}$ as terminology classes, while every operator is still a function as set-theoretic data.
items.definition: A semantic relation saying that 'function/map' imposes fewer assumptions, while 'operator' adds contextual structure such as vector-space, normed-space, or linear structure.
items.intuition: Function is the raw arrow. Operator is the arrow with structure attached. Same underlying species, different amount of mathematical baggage.

metadata.source: derived semantic clarification from standard functional-analysis convention

items.label: Closed subspace
items.statement: A closed subspace is a linear subspace that is closed in the ambient topology.
items.formal_statement: M\le X,\quad M=\overline{M}.
items.definition: A linear subspace containing all limits of convergent sequences from itself, in the ambient norm/topology.
items.intuition: A subspace with no missing limit points, so quotient constructions behave instead of collapsing into nonsense.
assumptions: X is a normed or topological vector space.
M is a linear subspace of X.

items.label: Quotient space
items.statement: Given a normed/Banach space X and a closed subspace Y, the quotient X/Y is the space of cosets x+Y.
items.formal_statement: X/Y=\{x+Y:x\in X\},\qquad \|x+Y\|=\inf_{y\in Y}\|x+y\|.
items.definition: The quotient space collapses a closed subspace to zero and measures distance to that subspace using the quotient norm.
items.intuition: You identify points that differ by an element of Y; geometry modulo a subspace, because apparently ordinary geometry was not abstract enough.
assumptions: X is a normed or Banach space.
Y is a closed linear subspace of X.

items.label: Quotient map
items.statement: The quotient map sends a vector to its coset.
items.formal_statement: Q:X\to X/Y,\qquad Qx=x+Y.
items.definition: The canonical surjective linear map from a space onto its quotient by a subspace.
items.intuition: Forget the component lying in Y.
assumptions: Y is a closed subspace of X.

items.label: Kernel
items.statement: The kernel of a linear operator is the set of vectors mapped to zero.
items.formal_statement: \ker T=\{x\in X:Tx=0\}.
items.definition: The null space of a linear operator.
items.intuition: Everything the operator kills.

items.label: Weak convergence
items.statement: A sequence converges weakly if every continuous linear functional converges on it.
items.formal_statement: x_n\rightharpoonup x \iff \forall x^*\in X^*:\ x^*(x_n)\to x^*(x).
items.definition: Weak convergence is convergence in the weak topology \sigma(X,X^*).
items.intuition: Vectors converge when every scalar probe in the dual agrees that they converge.
differentia: tested by all functionals in X*

items.label: Weak-star convergence
items.statement: A sequence of functionals converges weak-star if it converges pointwise on X.
items.formal_statement: x_n^*\overset{w^*}{\to}x^* \iff \forall x\in X:\ x_n^*(x)\to x^*(x).
items.definition: Weak-star convergence is convergence in \sigma(X^*,X).
items.intuition: Functionals converge by agreeing pointwise on every vector.
differentia: tested pointwise on X

items.label: Weakly bounded set
items.statement: A set is weakly bounded if every continuous linear functional is bounded on it.
items.formal_statement: A\subseteq X \text{ weakly bounded } \iff \forall x^*\in X^*:\ \sup_{a\in A}|x^*(a)|<\infty.
items.definition: Weak boundedness means boundedness after testing by every element of the dual space.
items.intuition: The set may look large in X, but every scalar probe sees only bounded values.
differentia: all continuous linear functionals are bounded on it

items.label: Weak boundedness equals norm boundedness in Banach spaces
items.statement: In a Banach space, every weakly bounded set is norm bounded.
items.formal_statement: X\text{ Banach},\ A\subseteq X,\ \forall x^*\in X^*:\sup_{a\in A}|x^*(a)|<\infty \implies \sup_{a\in A}\|a\|<\infty.
items.intuition: If every scalar probe sees boundedness, completeness upgrades this to actual norm boundedness.

items.label: Norm as a sublinear functional
items.statement: Every norm is a sublinear functional.
items.definition: The norm on a normed vector space is positively homogeneous and subadditive.
items.intuition: The norm is the basic domination function in Hahn-Banach.
assumptions: X is a normed vector space.

items.label: Nonlinearity
items.statement: A map is nonlinear if it fails additivity or homogeneity.
items.formal_statement: F(x+y)\ne F(x)+F(y) \text{ or } F(\lambda x)\ne \lambda F(x).
items.definition: Failure of linearity.
items.intuition: The map does not respect superposition.

items.label: Nonzero linear map on a normed space is not bounded as a function on the whole domain
items.statement: A nonzero bounded linear operator need not be bounded as a function on all of X.
items.formal_statement: T\ne0,\ T\in\mathcal L(X,Y) \implies \sup_{x\in X}\|Tx\|_Y=\infty.
items.definition: This distinguishes bounded linear operator from bounded function on its whole domain.
items.intuition: Linear maps scale. If one vector has nonzero image, scaling that vector makes the image arbitrarily large.
assumptions: X is a normed vector space over an unbounded scalar field.

items.label: Riesz Representation Theorem for C(K)
items.statement: The dual of C(K) is the space of finite Borel measures on K.
items.formal_statement: C(K)^*\cong M(K),\qquad \varphi(f)=\int_K f\,d\mu,\qquad \|\varphi\|=|\mu|(K).
items.intuition: Every continuous scalar probe on C(K) is integration against a measure.
assumptions: K is compact metric, in the Bowers-Kalton version.
items.conclusion: There exists a finite Borel measure \mu such that \varphi(f)=\int_K f\,d\mu.

completeness requires cauchy_sequence
edges.notes: Completeness is stated by requiring every Cauchy sequence to converge.

edges.label: metric_induced_by_norm induces norm-Cauchy sequences
edges.notes: In a normed vector space, the induced metric turns the metric Cauchy condition into $\|x_m-x_n\|\to 0$.

edges.label: normed_vector_space induces metric_induced_by_norm
edges.notes: A normed vector space has the canonical metric $d(x,y)=\|x-y\|$.

edges.label: banach_space is a subtype of normed_vector_space
edges.notes: A Banach space is a complete normed vector space.

edges.label: bounded_linear_operator requires operator_norm
edges.notes: The operator norm characterizes bounded linear operators by finiteness.

edges.label: bounded_linear_operator equivalent to continuous_linear_operator
edges.notes: For linear maps between normed vector spaces, boundedness and continuity are equivalent.

edges.label: bounded functions motivate comparison with bounded linear operators
edges.notes: The two notions use similar language but differ: bounded functions have bounded image, while bounded linear operators are controlled by input norm.

edges.label: function is prerequisite for bounded_function_on_domain
edges.notes: Boundedness on a domain is a property of a function into a normed space.

edges.label: function is prerequisite for operator
edges.notes: An operator is, at base, a function/map.

edges.label: function is prerequisite for bounded_function_on_domain as a definition
edges.notes: The bounded-function definition presupposes the general notion of function.

edges.label: function is prerequisite for linear_operator
edges.notes: A linear operator is a function satisfying linearity.

edges.label: operator is a subtype of function/map
edges.notes: Semantic hierarchy: operator is more specific than function.

edges.label: operator is prerequisite for linear_operator
edges.notes: Linearity is an additional condition imposed on an operator.

edges.label: operator is prerequisite for bounded_linear_operator
edges.notes: A bounded linear operator is an operator with linearity and boundedness.

edges.label: nonlinear_operator is a subtype of operator
edges.notes: Shows that operator is not universally synonymous with linear operator.

concept_map.description: Broad semantic map of Functional Analysis concepts without privileging a tree as the underlying structure.

definition_dependency_graph.description: Definitions needed to state concepts and theorems.

proof_graph.description: Proofs as first-class nodes with proof steps, methods, results, and critical assumptions.

inheritance_tree.description: Tree-like view extracted from subtype and inheritance edges; the graph itself still permits multiple inheritance.

construction_graph.description: Canonical constructions such as norm-to-metric, metric-to-topology, dual, weak topology, and adjoint.

prerequisite_map.description: Pedagogical order for learning, distinct from strict logical dependence.

application_map.description: Results and methods connected to their applications.

query_model.description: Query by filtering edges by type and dependency_class, then traversing outgoing, incoming, or both directions. Definitional dependencies state what is needed to formulate an item; assumption dependencies state hypotheses needed for truth; logical dependencies state proof/result dependencies; pedagogical dependencies state useful learning order.
query_model.node_lookup: Items are addressed by stable snake_case id. Edges are interpreted literally as source --type--> target.
query_model.transitive_closure: For edge types marked transitive, use recursive traversal when the query asks for full dependency closure or inheritance ancestors.
query_model.multigraph_rule: Multiple edges between the same source and target may coexist when they express different dependency classes or relations.

example_queries.example: Start at spectral_theorem; collect required definitions hilbert_space, compact_operator, self_adjoint_operator, spectrum, orthonormal_basis; logical tools riesz_representation_theorem, compactness_argument, approximation_argument; assumptions compact_self_adjoint_operator_assumption.

Start at open_mapping_theorem; traverse requires/definitional_dependency to banach_space, bounded_linear_operator, continuity; optionally close transitively to normed_vector_space, completeness, metric_induced_by_norm.
For hahn_banach_theorem, first find incoming proof --proves--> hahn_banach_theorem, then traverse proof_hahn_banach_zorn --uses--> duality_argument and zorns_lemma.
For uniform_boundedness_principle, outgoing assumes gives domain_is_banach_space and pointwise_bounded_family_assumption.
For banach_space, incoming instance_of returns ell_p_spaces, l_p_spaces, c_k, c_0, ell_2_as_hilbert_space; outgoing has_example returns the same representative examples.
For completeness, incoming shows_necessity_of finds incomplete_normed_spaces_as_non_examples_of_banach_spaces; then its related_theorems show why Banach-space completeness matters.

Starting at vector_space with incoming subtype_of gives normed_vector_space and topological_vector_space; from normed_vector_space gives banach_space and inner_product_space; hilbert_space has two outgoing subtype_of edges to inner_product_space and banach_space.

One path is vector_space -> normed_vector_space -> banach_space -> bounded_linear_operator -> compact_operator -> spectral_theorem; another is vector_space -> normed_vector_space -> inner_product_space -> hilbert_space -> self_adjoint_operator -> spectral_theorem.
